cards.definition: a quantity with magnitude only — e.g. speed, mass, energy, temperature
a quantity with magnitude and direction — e.g. velocity, force, acceleration, momentum
distance ÷ time — a scalar quantity
speed in a given direction — a vector. Velocity = displacement ÷ time
rate of change of velocity — a = (v−u)/t — units m/s²
gradient = velocity. Horizontal line = stationary. Curved line = changing speed
gradient = acceleration. Area under graph = distance travelled
object stays at rest or constant velocity unless acted on by a resultant force
F = ma — resultant force = mass × acceleration
every action has an equal and opposite reaction force — on different objects

questions.question: What is the difference between scalar and vector?
options: B) Vectors have direction; scalars have magnitude only
questions.explanation: Scalar: magnitude only (speed, mass, energy). Vector: magnitude AND direction (velocity, force, momentum).

questions.question: What does the gradient of a v-t graph represent?
questions.explanation: Gradient of a v-t graph = Δv/Δt = acceleration.

questions.question: What does the area under a v-t graph represent?
questions.explanation: Area under a v-t graph = velocity × time = distance travelled.

questions.question: What is Newton's Second Law?
options: A) Objects stay at rest unless forced
C) F = ma — resultant force = mass × acceleration
questions.explanation: F = ma. A larger force produces a greater acceleration for the same mass.

questions.question: What is Newton's Third Law?
options: B) Objects maintain constant velocity without force
C) Every action force has an equal and opposite reaction on a different object
questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law pairs: both forces are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, same type, but act on DIFFERENT objects.

questions.question: What is a vector quantity?
questions.explanation: Vectors need both a size and a direction to be fully described.

questions.question: What is acceleration?
options: C) Rate of change of velocity — (v−u)/t
questions.explanation: Acceleration can be positive (speeding up) or negative (decelerating). Units: m/s².

questions.question: What does a horizontal line on a d-t graph show?
options: B) Stationary — no change in displacement
questions.explanation: Horizontal line on a d-t graph: displacement not changing = stationary.

questions.question: What is Newton's First Law?
options: C) No resultant force → constant velocity or rest
D) Momentum is conserved in collisions
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law: without a resultant force, objects maintain their state of rest or uniform motion.

questions.question: What is velocity?
options: C) Speed in a given direction — displacement ÷ time
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector — it has both magnitude (speed) and direction. Displacement is also a vector.

questions.question: Velocity is a scalar quantity.
questions.explanation: Velocity is a VECTOR — it has both magnitude (speed) and direction.

questions.question: The gradient of a v-t graph gives acceleration.
questions.explanation: Gradient = Δv/Δt = acceleration.

questions.question: The area under a v-t graph gives distance.
questions.explanation: Area = velocity × time = distance.

questions.question: Newton's Second Law states F = ma.
questions.explanation: Resultant force = mass × acceleration.

questions.question: Newton's Third Law forces act on the same object.
questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law force pairs act on DIFFERENT objects.

questions.question: Mass is a vector quantity.
questions.explanation: Mass is a SCALAR — magnitude only.

questions.question: Acceleration can be negative.
questions.explanation: Negative acceleration means deceleration — the object is slowing down.

questions.question: A horizontal line on a d-t graph means constant velocity.
questions.explanation: Horizontal line on d-t graph = STATIONARY. Constant velocity on d-t = diagonal straight line.

questions.question: Newton's First Law requires a force to maintain constant velocity.
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law: NO force needed to maintain constant velocity — only to CHANGE it.

questions.question: Speed and velocity are the same quantity.
questions.explanation: Speed is scalar (magnitude only). Velocity is vector (magnitude + direction).

questions.question: A ________ quantity has magnitude only — e.g. speed, mass.
questions.explanation: No direction needed.

questions.question: A ________ quantity has magnitude and direction — e.g. velocity, force.
questions.explanation: Represented by arrows.

questions.question: ________ = speed in a given direction — displacement ÷ time.
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector; speed is a scalar.

questions.question: Gradient of a v-t graph = ________.
questions.explanation: Steeper gradient = greater acceleration.

questions.question: Area under a v-t graph = ________ travelled.
questions.explanation: Area = velocity × time.

questions.question: Newton's ________ Law: no resultant force → constant velocity or rest.
questions.explanation: A force is needed to CHANGE motion, not maintain it.

questions.question: Newton's ________ Law: F = ma.
questions.explanation: Greater force → greater acceleration for same mass.

questions.question: Newton's ________ Law: equal and opposite force pairs — on different objects.
questions.explanation: E.g. foot pushes ground → ground pushes foot upwards.

questions.question: ________ = rate of change of velocity — (v−u)/t — units m/s².
questions.explanation: Can be positive or negative.

questions.question: A horizontal line on a d-t graph means the object is ________.
questions.explanation: No change in displacement = not moving.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: magnitude and direction — e.g. velocity, force

cards.definition: the flow of charge — measured in amperes (A). I = Q/t
the work done per unit charge — measured in volts (V). V = W/Q
opposition to current flow — measured in ohms (Ω). R = V/I
V = IR — voltage is proportional to current at constant temperature for ohmic conductors
components connected in a single loop — same current throughout, voltages add up
components connected in branches — same voltage across each branch, currents add up
P = IV or P = I²R — rate of energy transfer — measured in watts (W)
build-up of charge on insulating materials — caused by friction transferring electrons
a region where a charge experiences a force — field lines go from + to −
current flowing in one direction only — e.g. from a battery

questions.question: What is Ohm's Law?
options: B) V = IR — voltage proportional to current at constant temperature
questions.explanation: Ohm's Law: V = IR. An ohmic conductor has constant resistance — the V-I graph is a straight line through the origin.

questions.question: What is potential difference?
options: B) Work done per unit charge — measured in volts
questions.explanation: Potential difference (voltage) = work done ÷ charge. V = W/Q. It drives current around the circuit.

questions.question: What happens to voltage in a series circuit?
options: A) Voltage is the same across each component
B) Voltages add up — total EMF shared between components
C) Voltage doubles across each component
questions.explanation: Series: voltages across components add up to the supply voltage. Current is the same everywhere.

questions.question: What happens to current in a parallel circuit?
options: A) Current is the same in each branch
B) Currents in each branch add up to the total current
C) Total current halves at each junction
D) Current is zero in parallel
questions.explanation: Parallel: current splits between branches — total current = sum of branch currents. Voltage is the same across each branch.

questions.question: What is the formula for electrical power?
options: B) P = IV or P = I²R
questions.explanation: Power = current × voltage (P = IV). Also P = I²R (useful when you know R and I).

questions.question: What causes static electricity?
options: A) Current flowing through a conductor
B) Friction transferring electrons between surfaces — charge builds up on insulators
questions.explanation: When two insulators rub together, electrons transfer — one becomes positively charged, one negatively charged.

questions.question: What is resistance?
options: B) Work done per unit charge
C) Opposition to current flow — measured in ohms (Ω)
questions.explanation: Resistance = voltage ÷ current (R = V/I). Higher resistance → less current for same voltage.

questions.question: What is direct current?
options: A) Current that changes direction periodically
B) Current that only flows in one direction — from a battery
C) A type of static electricity
questions.explanation: DC (direct current) flows in one direction — from batteries. AC (alternating current) changes direction — from the mains.

questions.question: What is the equation for current?
options: B) I = Q/t — charge per unit time
questions.explanation: Current (I) = charge (Q) ÷ time (t). Measured in amperes (A). Also I = V/R.

questions.question: What is an electric field?
options: A) A magnetic field around a conductor
B) A region where a charge experiences a force — field lines from + to −
C) The potential difference between two points
D) The resistance of a material
questions.explanation: Electric field lines show the direction a positive test charge would move — from positive to negative.

questions.question: Ohm's Law states V = IR.
questions.explanation: An ohmic conductor has a constant resistance — V-I graph is a straight line through origin.

questions.question: In a series circuit, current is different through each component.
questions.explanation: Series circuit: current is the SAME throughout.

questions.question: In a parallel circuit, voltage is the same across each branch.
questions.explanation: Parallel: same voltage across each branch. Current splits between branches.

questions.question: Power = current × voltage.
questions.explanation: P = IV. Also P = I²R and P = V²/R.

questions.question: Static electricity is caused by the flow of current.
questions.explanation: Static electricity is caused by FRICTION — electrons transfer between insulating surfaces.

questions.question: Resistance is measured in volts.
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in OHMS (Ω). Voltage is measured in volts.

questions.question: Direct current flows in one direction only.
questions.explanation: DC from batteries; AC from mains — AC changes direction.

questions.question: Voltages in series circuits add up to the supply voltage.
questions.explanation: Series: V₁ + V₂ + V₃ = total EMF.

questions.question: An electric field exerts a force on charged particles.
questions.explanation: Electric field lines show the direction of force on a positive charge.

questions.question: Current = charge ÷ time.
questions.explanation: I = Q/t. Current is the rate of flow of charge.

questions.question: ________ = V/I — opposition to current flow — measured in ohms.
questions.explanation: Higher resistance → less current for the same voltage.

questions.question: Ohm's Law: ________ = IR.
questions.explanation: Voltage is proportional to current for ohmic conductors.

questions.question: ________ = IV — rate of energy transfer — measured in watts.
questions.explanation: Also P = I²R.

questions.question: Series circuit: ________ is the same throughout.
questions.explanation: Voltages add up; current is constant.

questions.question: Parallel circuit: ________ is the same across each branch.
questions.explanation: Currents in branches add up to total current.

questions.question: ________ electricity is caused by friction transferring electrons.
questions.explanation: Builds up on insulators — can cause sparks.

questions.question: ________ current (DC) flows in one direction only — e.g. from batteries.
questions.explanation: AC (alternating current) comes from the mains.

questions.question: I = ________ ÷ time — charge flow per second.
questions.explanation: Measured in amperes (A).

questions.question: Electric field lines go from ________ to negative charge.
questions.explanation: The direction a positive test charge would move.

questions.question: Potential ________ is work done per unit charge — measured in volts.
questions.explanation: Also called voltage. V = W/Q.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: V = IR — voltage proportional to current
P = IV = I²R — rate of energy transfer
friction transfers electrons — charge on insulators

cards.definition: energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred between stores
W = Fd — force × distance — measured in joules
KE = ½mv² — energy of a moving object
GPE = mgh — energy due to height
useful energy output ÷ total energy input × 100%
a transfer of energy without transfer of matter — transverse or longitudinal
vibrations perpendicular to direction of travel — e.g. light, electromagnetic waves
vibrations parallel to direction of travel — e.g. sound, compressions and rarefactions
v = fλ — wave speed = frequency × wavelength
range of EM waves ordered by frequency/wavelength — radio to gamma rays

questions.question: What is conservation of energy?
options: A) Energy is created in nuclear reactions
B) Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred
C) Energy is always conserved in mechanical systems only
D) Energy decreases in every transfer
questions.explanation: Total energy in a closed system is always constant — energy changes form but is never created or destroyed.

questions.question: What is the formula for work done?
questions.explanation: Work done = force × distance (in direction of force). Units: joules (N⋅m).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
options: B) Energy stored in stretched objects
C) Energy of a moving object — KE = ½mv²
questions.explanation: KE = ½ × mass × velocity². Doubling speed quadruples KE (velocity is squared).

questions.question: What is efficiency?
options: B) Useful energy output ÷ total energy input × 100%
questions.explanation: Efficiency < 100% for all real machines — some energy is always dissipated as heat.

questions.question: What is a transverse wave?
options: A) Vibrations parallel to direction of travel
B) Vibrations perpendicular to direction of travel — e.g. light
C) A wave that transfers matter
questions.explanation: Transverse waves: vibrations at right angles to wave travel. Examples: all electromagnetic waves, water waves.

questions.question: What is a longitudinal wave?
options: A) Vibrations perpendicular to travel direction
B) Vibrations parallel to direction of travel — compressions and rarefactions
D) Waves that cannot travel through solids
questions.explanation: Longitudinal waves: vibrations in the same direction as travel. Sound is the main GCSE example.

questions.question: What is the wave speed equation?
questions.explanation: v = fλ. Wave speed = frequency (Hz) × wavelength (m). Speed in m/s.

questions.question: What is the electromagnetic spectrum?
options: B) A range of EM waves ordered by frequency — radio to gamma rays
C) Only waves that can travel through matter
D) A range of sound frequencies
questions.explanation: EM spectrum (low to high frequency): radio → microwave → infrared → visible → UV → X-ray → gamma.

questions.question: What is GPE?
options: C) Energy due to height — GPE = mgh
questions.explanation: GPE = mass × g × height. On Earth, g ≈ 10 N/kg. Increasing height increases GPE.

questions.question: What is the difference between transverse and longitudinal waves?
options: A) Only longitudinal waves transfer energy
B) Transverse: vibrations perpendicular; longitudinal: vibrations parallel to travel
C) Transverse waves cannot travel in a vacuum
D) Longitudinal waves have higher frequency
questions.explanation: Transverse: perpendicular vibrations (EM waves, water). Longitudinal: parallel vibrations (sound).

questions.question: Energy cannot be created or destroyed.
questions.explanation: Conservation of energy is a fundamental law of physics.

questions.question: Work done = force × time.
questions.explanation: Work done = force × DISTANCE (W = Fd).

questions.question: Doubling speed doubles kinetic energy.
questions.explanation: KE = ½mv² — speed is squared. Doubling speed QUADRUPLES KE.

questions.question: Efficiency can exceed 100%.
questions.explanation: Efficiency cannot exceed 100% — energy is always wasted as heat.

questions.question: Sound is a transverse wave.
questions.explanation: Sound is a LONGITUDINAL wave — vibrations are parallel to direction of travel.

questions.question: All electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed in a vacuum.
questions.explanation: All EM waves travel at 3 × 10⁸ m/s in a vacuum.

questions.question: Wave speed = frequency × wavelength.
questions.explanation: v = fλ.

questions.question: Gamma rays have the lowest frequency in the EM spectrum.
questions.explanation: Gamma rays have the HIGHEST frequency (and shortest wavelength).

questions.question: GPE increases with height.
questions.explanation: GPE = mgh — more height = more stored gravitational potential energy.

questions.question: Transverse waves have vibrations perpendicular to direction of travel.
questions.explanation: E.g. all EM waves, water waves. Sound is longitudinal.

questions.question: ________ of energy: cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred.
questions.explanation: Total energy in a closed system is constant.

questions.question: Work done = ________ × distance — measured in joules.
questions.explanation: W = Fd.

questions.question: KE = ½m________ — kinetic energy depends on speed squared.
questions.explanation: Doubling speed quadruples KE.

questions.question: GPE = m × ________ × height.
questions.explanation: On Earth, g ≈ 10 N/kg.

questions.question: ________ = useful output ÷ total input × 100%.
questions.explanation: All real machines are less than 100% efficient.

questions.question: ________ waves have vibrations perpendicular to direction of travel — e.g. light.
questions.explanation: All EM waves are transverse.

questions.question: ________ waves have vibrations parallel to direction of travel — e.g. sound.
questions.explanation: Sound travels as compressions and rarefactions.

questions.question: Wave speed = ________ × wavelength (v = fλ).
questions.explanation: Frequency in Hz, wavelength in m, speed in m/s.

questions.question: EM spectrum from lowest to highest frequency: radio → microwave → infrared → ________ → UV → X-ray → gamma.
questions.explanation: Visible light is a tiny part of the full EM spectrum.

questions.question: ________ rays have the highest frequency in the EM spectrum.
questions.explanation: Gamma rays also have the shortest wavelength.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

cards.definition: the turning effect of a force — moment = force × perpendicular distance from pivot (N⋅m)
for a balanced object: sum of clockwise moments = sum of anticlockwise moments
the point where all of an object's weight appears to act — determines stability
P = F/A — pressure increases with depth in liquids (liquid pressure = ρgh)
the upward force on an object submerged in a fluid — equal to weight of fluid displaced
pressure exerted by the weight of air above — decreases with altitude
planets orbit the Sun due to gravitational attraction — centripetal force provided by gravity
light from distant galaxies is shifted towards the red end of the spectrum — evidence for expansion
the Universe began as an extremely dense, hot point — it has been expanding ever since
nebula → protostar → main sequence → red giant/supergiant → white dwarf/supernova/neutron star/black hole

questions.question: What is a moment?
options: B) The turning effect of a force — force × perpendicular distance from pivot
questions.explanation: Moment (N⋅m) = force (N) × perpendicular distance from pivot (m). A longer spanner produces a greater moment.

questions.question: What is the principle of moments?
options: A) All forces must be equal
B) Clockwise moments = anticlockwise moments for a balanced object
C) The centre of mass must be at the pivot
D) Forces always act through the centre of mass
questions.explanation: Principle of moments: for equilibrium, sum of clockwise moments about any point = sum of anticlockwise moments.

questions.question: What is upthrust?
options: A) The weight of an object in a fluid
B) The upward force equal to the weight of fluid displaced
C) The pressure at the surface of a fluid
questions.explanation: Upthrust = weight of fluid displaced (Archimedes' principle). If upthrust > weight, the object floats.

questions.question: What is red-shift?
options: A) Stars appearing red due to their temperature
B) Light from distant galaxies shifted towards longer wavelengths — evidence the Universe is expanding
C) A type of nuclear radiation
D) The light from cooling stars
questions.explanation: Red-shift: the further a galaxy, the greater the red-shift — evidence that the Universe is expanding in all directions.

questions.question: What is the Big Bang theory?
options: B) The Universe began as an extremely hot, dense point and has been expanding ever since
C) Matter being created in stars
D) The theory of nuclear fusion
questions.explanation: Big Bang theory is supported by red-shift and cosmic microwave background radiation (CMBR).

questions.question: What is atmospheric pressure?
options: A) Pressure from a liquid column
B) The pressure exerted by the weight of air — decreases with altitude
D) The pressure at the centre of the Earth
questions.explanation: Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude — less air above = less weight = less pressure.

questions.question: What force provides centripetal force for planetary orbits?
options: C) Gravitational attraction between the planet and the Sun
questions.explanation: Gravity provides the centripetal force — planets are constantly pulled towards the Sun, keeping them in orbit.

questions.question: What is pressure in a liquid?
options: B) P = F/A — increases with depth (P = ρgh)
questions.explanation: Liquid pressure: P = ρgh (density × g × height). Pressure increases with depth.

questions.question: What happens to a star like our Sun after the main sequence?
options: A) It becomes a black hole
B) It explodes as a supernova
C) It becomes a red giant then a white dwarf
D) It becomes a neutron star
questions.explanation: Sun-like stars: main sequence → red giant → planetary nebula → white dwarf. More massive stars end as supernovae → neutron star or black hole.

questions.question: What is the principle of moments used for?
options: B) Finding when a balanced object will topple
C) Calculating turning effects — for balanced beams and levers
questions.explanation: Principle of moments: if Σ clockwise moments = Σ anticlockwise moments, the object is in rotational equilibrium.

questions.question: Moment = force × perpendicular distance from pivot.
questions.explanation: Units: N⋅m. Longer distance from pivot = greater moment.

questions.question: For a balanced object, clockwise moments exceed anticlockwise moments.
questions.explanation: For BALANCE: clockwise moments = anticlockwise moments.

questions.question: Upthrust is the downward force on a submerged object.
questions.explanation: Upthrust is UPWARD — equal to the weight of fluid displaced.

questions.question: Red-shift provides evidence that the Universe is expanding.
questions.explanation: Galaxies moving away cause red-shift — the further the galaxy, the greater the red-shift.

questions.question: Atmospheric pressure increases with altitude.
questions.explanation: Atmospheric pressure DECREASES with altitude — less air above.

questions.question: Gravity provides the centripetal force for planetary orbits.
questions.explanation: Without gravity, planets would travel in straight lines.

questions.question: Liquid pressure increases with depth.
questions.explanation: P = ρgh — more depth = more liquid above = more pressure.

questions.question: Our Sun will end its life as a black hole.
questions.explanation: The Sun will become a RED GIANT then a WHITE DWARF. Black holes form from much more massive stars.

questions.question: The Big Bang theory is supported by red-shift evidence.
questions.explanation: Red-shift and CMBR both support the Big Bang model.

questions.question: The principle of moments applies when a system is in rotational equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Σ clockwise moments = Σ anticlockwise moments.

questions.question: ________ = force × perpendicular distance from pivot — turning effect.
questions.explanation: Units: N⋅m.

questions.question: Principle of moments: ________ moments = anticlockwise moments for balance.
questions.explanation: Used to solve beam and lever problems.

questions.question: ________ is the upward force on a submerged object — weight of fluid displaced.
questions.explanation: If upthrust > weight, object floats.

questions.question: ________ shows light from galaxies is shifted to longer wavelengths — Universe expanding.
questions.explanation: Greater distance = greater red-shift.

questions.question: ________ Bang theory: Universe began as hot dense point — expanding since.
questions.explanation: Supported by red-shift and cosmic microwave background radiation.

questions.question: ________ pressure = weight of air above — decreases with altitude.
questions.explanation: Less air above at high altitude = lower pressure.

questions.question: ________ provides centripetal force for planets orbiting the Sun.
questions.explanation: Without gravity, planets would travel in straight lines.

questions.question: Liquid pressure P = ρg________ — increases with depth.
questions.explanation: ρ = density, g = gravitational field strength, h = depth.

questions.question: Sun-like stars: red giant → planetary nebula → ________ dwarf.
questions.explanation: More massive stars end as neutron stars or black holes.

questions.question: The life cycle of a star begins with a ________.
questions.explanation: A nebula is a cloud of gas and dust that collapses under gravity.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: turning effect — force × perpendicular distance from pivot
upward force = weight of fluid displaced
evidence Universe is expanding — galaxies moving away
Universe from hot dense point — still expanding
